texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reverse osmosis (RO) is a widely used technology to desalinate water. In this work, several methods are proposed to increase the recovery of RO. The proposed methods are hybrid systems with a combination of RO, chemical deposition (CD) coupled with ultra fi ltration (UF), nano fi ltration (NF) and membrane distillation (MD). The proposed setups were assessed on a techno-economic level. The MD lab tests showed scaling on the membranes which indicate that pre-treatment is needed for treating RO-brine. Secondly, the proposed methods were tested on a pilot-scale to determine the maximum recovery of each system. The sensitivity analysis shows that the cost of water (COW) is mostly in fl uenced by the total water production, which in fl uences the capital cost. Therefore, more work is needed to reduce the capital cost of MD. When adding antiscalant to RO + MD, a base case COW of 0.633 USD/m 3 and overall recovery of 84.59% was reached. The other setups had lower recovery, 66.93%, 72.73% and 73.38% for RO + CD + UF + MD, RO + NF + MD and RO + NF + antiscalant +MD, respectively, while the base case COW of these setups were 0.692, 0.7 and 0.698 USD/m 3 , respectively.

texts.orig: and monovalent ions from the water leading to the term called desalination. Examples of desalination with thermal methods are multistage fl ash distillation (MSF), multiple-e ff ect distillation (MED), and membrane distillation (MD) [6]. However, about 80% of the total desalination plants are based on RO [7], partly due to the low cost of operation and its simplicity [8]. RO produces 65.5 million m 3 of desalinated water per day at this moment [9]. However, the speci fi c energy needed by RO systems increases signi fi cantly at high salinities due to the higher osmotic pressure they encompass. Therefore, RO is not used in applications with a high salinity [8,10]. To avoid working at high salinities, seawater RO recovery is around 45 -50%, leading to the production of a brine stream whose volume is similar to the produced permeate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) the brine is usually discharged back to the sea, and always has a higher concentration than the seawater. This discharged water also contains chemicals used for pretreatment to prevent biofouling which reduces the clean water output [11 -15]. The brine can be discharged with surface water discharge, deep well injection, and evaporations ponds [12]. New methods should be developed to minimize the brine discharge and increase the sustainability of the process. The most environmental friendly method is zero liquid discharge (ZLD) where only desalinated water and dry salts are produced [16]. The bene fi ts of ZLD are maximum water recovery, the preservation of natural resources and the by-product salt that can be classi fi ed as added value [17]. However, ZLD is currently characterized as energy and cost-intensive [18].

texts.orig: Another way to deal with the produced brine is to couple RO with other technologies like UF, NF or MD, in order to increase the water recovery. For example, in the work of Tun and Growth, a fl ux of 3 -5l/ m 2 ∙ h and a water recovery of 75 -80% was achieved with treating RO brine with MD [19]. In the work of Mericq et al., a recovery of 89% was reached with a concentration factor of 5.5 with the application of SWRO and VMD [20]. In the work of Duong et al., a recovery of 95% was reached from treating coal seam gas (CSG) produced water by UF/ RO and MD hybrid system [21].

texts.orig: The recovery can be increased when the technologies are coupled. This method is tested in the European project named ' Dreamer ' which is part of LIFE [22]. An overview of the proposed setup is shown in Fig. 1. The phosphate adsorption is added to prevent biological fouling by reducing the nutrients. Bio fi lm prevention by phosphate adsorption has already been studied at a small scale [23,24]. The chemical deposition will be fi ltered out by using ultra fi ltration, and then, the treated seawater will go in the RO unit. The brine discharge will then pass nano fi ltration to remove the divalent ions to prevent scaling in the membrane distillation unit. Moreover, antiscalants can be added to further reduce the risk of scaling. The expected result of this setup is an increase in water recovery to over 90% [22]. Furthermore, the proposed setup of Fig. 1 has a lower use of chemicals and lower speci fi c energy consumption compared to conventional RO.

texts.orig: To reach 90% water recovery, RO, CD-UF, NF and MD will be used. One-pass SWRO systems usually have a recovery of 35 -60% [6]. In the Life|Dreamer project, RO has a water recovery of 50% [22], thus, MD should have a water recovery of 60%. The fi nal 10% will result from cleaning the water e ffl uents. The setup that is investigated in the ' Dreamer ' project is CD-UF-RO-NF-MD, however, more combinations are possible.

texts.orig: In this paper, several combinations of RO with MD, NF, and CD-UF will be assessed on a technical and an economic level. The chosen combinations are RO-MD with and without antiscalant, RO-CD-UF-MD and RO-NF-MD with and without antiscalant, which are schematically illustrated in Fig. 2. CD-UF will be placed after the RO, instead of before the RO, in order to remove the bivalent ions as the bio fi lm prevention is not tested in this report.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several tests were performed in this study, which are schematically shown in Table 1. To determine if pre-treatment is needed, the RO brine water was treated on an MD lab-scale setup. If scaling occurred on the membrane, pre-treatment is needed. Two possibilities for pre-treatment of SWRO brine were considered: The fi rst setup is treating RO brine with chemical deposition (CD) followed by ultra fi ltration (UF), which was assessed in test 5. The second setup is treating RO brine with nano fi ltration (NF) with and without the use of antiscalant, which was assessed in test 6. In test 2, a membrane was chosen for NF. In test 4, the maximum recovery of a pilot MD system with antiscalant was assessed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To determine if pre-treatment is needed a lab-scale test was performed on direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) with RO brine. Three preliminary tests were performed with the test setup that is shown in Fig. 3, whose membrane area is 0.05m 2 . The tests were performed at 45 °C, 65 °C and 80 °C with an inlet fl ow of 100 l/h. The time for experiments 1,2 and 3 was 49 h, 75 h, and 33 h, respectively. The fl ow was measured with Burkert 8031. The pressures were measured with Burkert 8316 and JUMO Midas S06. The temperatures were measured with type 74WTH temperature sensor from Günther.

texts.orig: The tests were done at Aqua|still with a pilot scale MD installation, with a brine tank size of approximately 170 l. AS7, AS26, and AS24 modules were used which have 1.5 m, 2.7 m, and 5 m long envelopes, respectively. All three units were spiral wound vacuum-assisted air gap membrane distillation (VA-AGMD). The di ff erence between VA-AGMD and AGMD is that a vacuum is applied in the air gap to reduce the mass and heat resistance. The setup is shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5. The pilot consists of two heat exchangers to heat up, and cool down the water.

texts.orig: that is placed in front of the hot entry of the MD module. Scaling occurs at the highest or lowest temperatures of the system, which is in this case, the hot heat exchanger and the pipework. The deposition increases the pressure drop of the system without scaling the membrane or decreasing the distillate fl ux. The second e ff ect that occurs is the decrease of distillate fl ux due to membrane scaling, which happens shortly after the fi rst e ff ect. The third and last e ff ect is a decrease in distillate quality. This is due to the penetration of salt crystal through the membrane. This can also happen when a module is not used for a long time and all the water has evaporated. This results in salt drying and penetrating the membrane. However, the third e ff ect has not yet been found in literature. An MD module should be treated to remove the scaling after the second e ff ect is observed. Therefore, the maximum concentration in the test was reached when the fi rst e ff ect took place.

texts.orig: Scaling was observed by visual analysis on the membrane surface in experiment 2 and 3, which can be seen in Fig. 11. The scaling leaves a yellowish taint on the white membrane as can be observed in Fig. 12. On the left of Fig. 11, the result of test 1 can be seen. The two squares on the membrane come from the tape that was used to hold the membrane in place. The small prints are not from scaling but are plastic deformation due to the pressing of the spacer on the membrane. On the right side of Fig. 11, slight scaling can be seen on the membrane. On the left side of the membrane, more scaling can be seen which corresponds to the location of the hot feed entry. The cold entry is located on the right, were limited scaling was found. Therefore, since scaling was already observed in a short period of time, pre-treatment of the SWRO brine is needed for MD application to prevent loss in production and a rise in energy consumption for a long-term operation.

texts.orig: To select the optimal pressure of the NF-unit the speci fi c energy consumption was calculated. In Fig. 14 the speci fi c energy consumption is plotted against the feed pressure. For a system, a low energy consumption but high recovery is preferred. Therefore, the input pressure of the NF should be around 10 bar. This way, a recovery of 30% can be reached. As the pressure drop of one NF module was around 0.7 bar, the input pressure should rise to 15 bar. This way, an optimal system was achieved.

texts.orig: The average fl ux, average GOR, and concentration factor are shown in Table 11 for the MD experiments. The results indicate that the maximum fl ux was achieved at a cross fl ow velocity of 0.022 m / s , a hot feed temperature of 85 °C, and a pressure of 0.5 Pa in the air gap. The pressure in the air gap was reduced to 0.5 Pa absolute, this way there is less mass resistance in the air gap. The maximum GOR is reached at a cross fl ow velocity of 0.011 m/s, a hot feed temperature of 85 °C and a pressure of 0.5 Pa in the air gap. The maximum concentration factor was reached at the test of C8 with a value of 2.67 with a maximum TDS of 173,536 ppm.

texts.orig: In the work of Loutatidou et al. a regression model was made to determine the engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) cost of an RO plant in the GCC region and in the south of Europe [33]. The parameters of this EPC equation are the type of capacity, type of seawater, region, and the year of commissioning. In the work of [34] a function was estimated for the capital cost of RO plants by using ordinary least square method. The equations are listed in Table 20. The formula for EPC is used as the site development is usually already considered. 55% of the desalination plants are in GCC countries [35]. Therefore, the GCC region will be used in this assessment. Moreover, the di ff erence between the GCC and southern European countries only a ff ects the cost of water with 0.04 USD/m 3 for a plant with a capacity of 100 m 3 /day.

texts.text: The maintenance cost of an MD plant is 1.3% of the MD CAPEX [47]. A value of 2.5% of the capital cost was also found for service and maintenance [10,46]. The higher value of 2.5% will be used as this is more common in literature. An additional cost of 0.22 USD/m 3 for the module replacement will be used which corresponds to the module changing every 5 years. The modules need to be replaced to prevent loss of production due to the deterioration of the membranes. The overview of the cost of MD can be seen in Table 23 and Table 24.

texts.text: 4.3. Benchmarking of the methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A setup with a high recovery and low cost of water is preferred. The RO + MD has the lowest cost of water and the highest recovery. However, this setup cannot be used as this setup has scaling issues. However, when adding antiscalant the recovery of RO is the highest while having lower COW than the UF and NF counterparts. In all the setups the cost of water is mostly in fl uenced by the capital cost. Therefore, to make the proposed pre-treatment economically feasible the CAPEX should decrease. As MD is still undergoing major developments, more work is needed to reduce the capital cost of membrane distillation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, several methods were proposed for pre-treating the RO brine for MD applications. The MD lab test found scaling after running with SWRO brine. Therefore, pre-treatment is needed to treat RO brine with MD. Several pre-treatment methods were proposed: RO + MD with antiscalant, RO + CD + UF + MD and RO + NF + MD with and without antiscalant dose. All the proposed methods were tested in an MDpilot with a capacity of 2.5 m 3 /day (in nominal conditions for clean water). The method that used ultra fi ltration had the lowest recovery with a cost comparable to the nano fi ltration methods. However, due to all the chemicals that were added in the chemical deposition, the starting concentration was higher than the other methods. The method with the highest recovery is RO + MD with antiscalant.
texts.text: In this work, several methods were proposed for pre-treating the RO brine for MD applications. The MD lab test found scaling after running with SWRO brine. Therefore, pre-treatment is needed to treat RO brine with MD. Several pre-treatment methods were proposed: RO + MD with antiscalant, RO + CD + UF + MD and RO + NF + MD with and without antiscalant dose. All the proposed methods were tested in an MDpilot with a capacity of 2.5 m 3 /day (in nominal conditions for clean water). The method that used ultra fi ltration had the lowest recovery with a cost comparable to the nano fi ltration methods. However, due to all the chemicals that were added in the chemical deposition, the starting concentration was higher than the other methods. The method with the highest recovery is RO + MD with antiscalant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The sensitivity analyses on the water cost revealed that the biggest change in cost comes from the total water production and the electricity price for MD and RO, respectively. If the total water production is lowered, the cost of MD becomes higher due to the lower number of modules in respect to the auxiliaries, like the electrical components of a system. The proposed setups were most sensitive to the total water production and the electricity price as the added cost of NF and UF are small compared to the cost of MD and RO. The base case cost of RO + MD was lower than all the other methods and has the highest recovery. Therefore, for the base case, RO + MD with antiscalant is the best option for treating seawater with MD. The overall recovery and cost of water of RO + MD with antiscalant was found to be 84.5% and 0.633 USD/m 3 , respectively. Note that 0.633 USD/m 3 is for the base case and can changes as other values were chosen in the sensitivity analysis.
